---
title: "Replication of Card & Krueger (1994)"
author: "Amelia Li"
date: today
categories: [econometrics, DID]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## Introduction

Does raising the minimum wage reduce employment? Standard economic theory predicts that higher minimum wages lead to lower employment. However, Card and Krueger (1994) challenge this view using a natural experiment: the increase in New Jersey’s minimum wage in 1992, while neighboring Pennsylvania did not change its policy.

This study compares employment changes in fast-food restaurants across the two states using a difference-in-differences (DID) approach.

---

## Data

The dataset consists of fast-food restaurants in:

- New Jersey (treatment group)
- Pennsylvania (control group)

Observed at two points:
- Before (Feb 1992)
- After (Nov 1992)

---

## Main Result: Difference-in-Differences

The DID model is:

$$
Y_{it} = \alpha + \beta Treat_i + \gamma Post_t + \delta (Treat_i \times Post_t) + \epsilon_{it}
$$

Where:
- $Treat_i = 1$ for NJ
- $Post_t = 1$ after policy
- $\delta$ is the treatment effect

---

## Replication (Using Summary Statistics)

From the paper:

- NJ before: 20.4  
- NJ after: 21.0  
- PA before: 23.3  
- PA after: 21.2  

NJ_before <- 20.4
NJ_after  <- 21.0
PA_before <- 23.3
PA_after  <- 21.2

DID <- (NJ_after - NJ_before) - (PA_after - PA_before)
DID

Result
The DID estimate is computed as:
(21.0 - 20.4) - (21.2 - 23.3) = 2.7
The DID estimate is approximately:2.76
This matches the main finding of the paper.

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

df <- data.frame(
  State = rep(c("New Jersey", "Pennsylvania"), each = 2),
  Period = rep(c("Before", "After"), times = 2),
  Employment = c(20.4, 21.0, 23.3, 21.2)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = Period, y = Employment, group = State, color = State)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Before", "After")) +
  labs(
    title = "Employment Before and After Minimum Wage Increase",
    y = "FTE Employment"
  )
```

## Interpretation:
Pennsylvania employment decreased
New Jersey employment slightly increased
The difference between these trends is exactly the DID estimate.

## Discussion

The results suggest that increasing the minimum wage did not reduce employment, contrary to traditional theory.

This causal interpretation relies on the parallel trends assumption:
Without the policy, NJ and PA would have followed similar trends.

## Extension

We can also interpret the DID estimate as:
Employment increased by about 2.76 workers per store
Roughly a 13% increase relative to baseline

## Conclusion

This replication confirms the main finding of Card & Krueger (1994):
there is no evidence that the minimum wage increase reduced employment.

The study demonstrates the power of quasi-experimental methods like DID in evaluating policy effects.
